coredata.dc:title: AgBiS2 nanoparticles with synergistic photodynamic and bioimaging properties for enhanced malignant tumor phototherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Jing
Wang, Wanshun
Xu, Xingyi
Lin, Zefeng
Xie, Chao
Zhang, Yuxuan
Zhang, Tao
Li, Lihua
Lu, Yao
Li, Qi
coredata.dc:description: Bismuth (Bi)-based nanoagents for synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are attracting attention and are highly desired for malignant tumor diagnosis and treatment, but producing these materials is still a challenge. Here, we designed theranostic nanoparticles (NPs) based on AgBiS2 for computed tomography (CT) imaging and phototherapy of malignant tumors. These AgBiS2 NPs could effectively convert light into heat (with a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 36.51%) and significantly increase the generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) under near infrared (NIR) laser irradiation. Remarkably, the combined PTT/PDT successfully inhibited the growth of a highly malignant osteosarcoma in vivo. In addition, AgBiS2 NPs exhibited an enhanced CT contrast ability for tumor imaging and killed clinically derived Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) to prevent infection. In conclusion the ability of AgBiS2 NPs to be used in phototherapy and CT imaging and their antibacterial abilities indicate that they are promising candidates for malignant tumor theranostics.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility and tumor ablation in vitro
In vitro and in vivo CT imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by China Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Supporting Program ( BX20190150 ), Presidential Foundation of the Zhujiang Hospital ( yzjj2018rc09 ), Scientific Research Foundation of Southern Medical University ( C1051353 , PY2018N060 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M640776 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81601884 , 81902198 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province ( 2014A020215025 , 2017B030314139 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2015A030312004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the AgBiS2 NPs. (a, b) TEM images of the AgBiS2 NPs. (c) HRTEM image of the AgBiS2 NPs. (d) SAED pattern of the AgBiS2 NPs. (e) XRD pattern of the AgBiS2 sample and JCPDS standard pattern of a AgBiS2 crystal. (f) UV-vis spectra of the AgBiS2 NPs.

Photothermal and photodynamic effect of the AgBiS2 NPs. (a) Quantitative temperature change induced by the AgBiS2 NPs with different concentrations under laser irradiation (808 nm, 1 W cm−2). (b) Heating and cooling experiment performed with the AgBiS2 NPs solution (1000 μg mL−1) under 1 W cm−2 808 nm laser irradiation. The laser was turned on from 0 to 600 s and then shut off from 600 to 1200 s. The PTC efficiency (η) was calculated to be 36.51%. (c) IR images of the osteosarcoma-bearing mice captured every 2 min (from left to right) showed the temperature changes at the tumor site under NIR (808 nm, 1 W cm−2) irradiation. The light was only applied to the tumor during the measurements after intratumoral injection of the AgBiS2 (top) and NaCl (control, bottom), respectively. (d) H2O2 concentration released by the UMR-106 cells after different treatments. H2O2 generated over time only in the AgBiS2 + NIR group. (e) Fluorescence images of the intracellular singlet oxygen production (green fluorescence), which was observed only in the AgBiS2 + NIR group. (f) Fluorescence images of the intracellular ROS generation (green fluorescence), which was significantly higher in the AgBiS2 + NIR group. Data were represented as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3); *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. Scale bar = 100 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Biocompatibility and tumor ablation in vitro. (a) Cell viability of the UMR-106 cells co-cultured with different concentrations of the AgBiS2 NPs for 24 h. (b) Hemolysis test of human blood exposed to the AgBiS2 NPs. The inset image shows the samples after centrifugation. (c) Cell viability after different treatments. (d) Fluorescence images after Live/Dead staining the UMR-106 cells in the different groups. Each value is the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3); *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. Scale bar = 100 μm.

In vivo therapy effect of the different treatment groups. (a) Representative images of the osteosarcoma-bearing mice 1, 7 and 14 days after the different treatments. The tumors are highlighted by the red arrows. (b) Images of the tumors collected from the mice 14 days after the different treatments. (c) Tumor volume growth curves of the different treatment groups. (d) Body weight changes observed in the different treatment groups. Each value is the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3); *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Histological study. (a) H&E staining of tumors after different treatments. (b) H&E staining of the main organs of the different treatment groups. Scale bar = 200 μm.

CT imaging performance of the AgBiS2 NPs. (a) In vitro CT value (HU) of the AgBiS2 NPs. The inset image shows the CT images of the AgBiS2 NPs with various concentrations. (b) In vivo CT images of the NPs. Enhanced CT contrast was found at the tumor site (highlighted by the red circle) after the NPs were injected. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Antibacterial properties of the AgBiS2 NPs. (a) Clinical S. aureus colonies in the presence of the NPs. (b) Counts of the S. aureus colonies. (c) Antibacterial rate of the AgBiS2 NPs with different concentrations. Each value is the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3); *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 compared to the control group.
Schematic illustration of the applications of the multifunctional AgBiS2 NPs.

head.ce:title: AgBiS2 nanoparticles with synergistic photodynamic and bioimaging properties for enhanced malignant tumor phototherapy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Orthopedics, Zhujiang Hospital, Southern Medical University, 253 Gongye Road, Guangzhou, Guangdong, 510282, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bismuth (Bi)-based nanoagents for synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) are attracting attention and are highly desired for malignant tumor diagnosis and treatment, but producing these materials is still a challenge. Here, we designed theranostic nanoparticles (NPs) based on AgBiS2 for computed tomography (CT) imaging and phototherapy of malignant tumors. These AgBiS2 NPs could effectively convert light into heat (with a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 36.51%) and significantly increase the generation of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) under near infrared (NIR) laser irradiation. Remarkably, the combined PTT/PDT successfully inhibited the growth of a highly malignant osteosarcoma in vivo. In addition, AgBiS2 NPs exhibited an enhanced CT contrast ability for tumor imaging and killed clinically derived Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) to prevent infection. In conclusion the ability of AgBiS2 NPs to be used in phototherapy and CT imaging and their antibacterial abilities indicate that they are promising candidates for malignant tumor theranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Bismuth (Bi)-based nanomaterials (NMs) are outstanding candidates for computed tomography (CT) guided photothermal agents and have attracted considerable interest in tumor research. Due to their broad absorption in the NIR region, Bi-based NMs exhibit excellent photothermal effects [1]. Moreover, Bi has a large atomic number (83) and a high X-ray attenuation coefficient, so Bi-based NMs are attractive new CT contrast agents for tumor imaging [2]. Recently, different compositions and shapes of Bi-based NMs, such as Bi2S3, Bi2Se3, and Cu3BiS3 NMs, have been explored for tumor theranostics [3–5]. These reports have demonstrated that Bi-based NMs have great potential for in vivo tumor photothermal therapy (PTT) and CT imaging applications. However, the therapeutic efficiency of PTT alone cannot be exploited for ablating highly malignant tumors (e.g., an osteosarcoma) [6]. Therefore, the development of new Bi-based NMs with synergistic therapeutic effects and a good CT imaging ability for malignant tumor treatment is highly desired.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is another type of laser-triggered method that damages tumor cells by producing reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as singlet oxygen and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [7,8]. To achieve multiple therapeutic effects, Bi2S3 composites and heterostructures have emerged. Guo et al. have combined bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) with Bi2S3 NPs to synthesize a BiOI@Bi2S3 semiconductor and achieved three therapeutic effects (PTT, PDT, and radiotherapy) [9]. Very recently, Cheng et al. have linked the PDT drug zinc protoporphyrin IX (ZP) to Bi2S3 nanorods through a thermoresponsive polymer to form BPZP nanosystems [10]. The final products can generate ROS due to the facilitation of electron-hole separation in ZP and Bi2S3, which results in combined PTT/PDT. However, the synthesis of these materials is tedious. To date, few ternary Bi-based NMs have been reported to exhibit dual PTT/PDT effects, and few of these NMs, e.g., bismuth oxyhalide (BiOCl), bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6), and bismuth ferrite (BFO), can be easily prepared [11–13]. Hence, enriching such Bi-based NMs with dual PTT and PDT capabilities remains a challenge.

Here, we prepared unique AgBiS2 NPs by a facile solvothermal method. After Tween-20 modification, the AgBiS2 NPs exhibited good biocompatibility in vitro and in vivo. Under NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W cm-2), the AgBiS2 NPs presented with an excellent photothermal ability (η = 36.51%) and increased the generation ROS (H2O2 and singlet oxygen). Interestingly, this synergistic PTT/PDT successfully ablated the tumor cells in vitro and the highly malignant osteosarcoma in vivo. In addition, due to the presence of Bi and Ag, the NPs could serve as a CT imaging agent by enhancing the CT contrast at the tumor site and effectively eliminated the clinically derived Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) to prevent bacterial infection during treatment. Hence, the multifunctional AgBiS2 NPs-based PTT/PDT has a bright future for malignant tumor therapy (Scheme 1 ).

The AgBiS2 NPs were synthesized via a facile solvothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a and b) showed the fingerprint-like AgBiS2 NPs with a size of ≈ 80 nm. Fig. 1c presents the high-resolution TEM image of the AgBiS2 microstructure, and the interplanar crystal spacing is ≈ 0.28 nm. This interplanar spacing corresponds to the d-spacing of the (200) planes of the AgBiS2 nanocrystals. The polycrystalline structure of the interplanar AgBiS2 NPs is shown in Fig. 1d. The crystallinity and phase structure of the AgBiS2 NPs were studied by XRD (Fig. 1e). All the peaks in the XRD pattern were indexed to standard orthorhombic AgBiS2 crystal phases (JCPDS 21-1178), suggesting the high purity of the NPs. These results demonstrated the successful synthesis of the unique AgBiS2 NPs.

A UV-vis-NIR spectroscopic analysis was conducted to analyze the NIR absorption ability of the AgBiS2 NPs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the NPs exhibited a broad absorption from the visible region to the NIR region, which is similar to the characteristic of bismuth sulfide [6,14] and suggests the potential photothermal effect of the NPs. We measured the photothermal effect of the AgBiS2 NPs by applying NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W cm-2). After 10 min of irradiation, the temperature of the AgBiS2 solution (1000 μg mL−1) reached up to 62 °C (Fig. 2 a). Moreover, the temperature could still reach 49 °C even when the NPs concentration was as low as 125 μg mL−1. In contrast, DI water only reached 29 °C under the same conditions. Then, we measured the photothermal conversion (PTC) efficiency according to the results of the heating and cooling experiment (Fig. 2b), and the detailed method is presented in Fig. S1 and the Supporting Information. Impressively, the PTC efficiency (η) of the AgBiS2 NPs was calculated to be 36.51%. This photothermal ability was better than that of conventional noble gold (Au) NPs [15,16], whose PTC efficiency is approximately 23, which was also relatively high amongst the present photothermal materials [17–19].

Encouraged by the outstanding photothermal performance of the NPs, we investigated the photothermal effects in osteosarcoma-bearing mice. After the intratumoral injection of the AgBiS2 NPs (5 mg kg-1) and the application of NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W cm−2, 10 min), the temperature at the tumor site increased to 51 °C (Fig. 2c). On the contrary, the tumor site temperature was lower than 40 °C after 10 min of irradiation in the control group (NaCl + NIR). These above-mentioned results demonstrated that the AgBiS2 NPs could effectively convert NIR light into heat. Generally, temperatures above 41 °C cause cell inactivation for several hours, and cell inactivation becomes irreversible when the temperature is greater than 48 °C [20]. Hence, the AgBiS2 NPs can serve as ideal PTT agents. Recently, Lei et al. have prepared polyethylenimine-protected AgBiS2 nanodots for tumor dual-mode imaging guided PTT [21]. However, the photodynamic performance of the AgBiS2 NPs needs further investigation.

To investigate the photodynamic effects of the AgBiS2 NPs, we tested the release of H2O2 from UMR-106 cells and the generation of intracellular singlet oxygen and ROS. The changes in the released H2O2 concentration are shown in Fig. 2d. Obviously, the H2O2 concentration increased over time only in the AgBiS2 + NIR group, indicating that the production of H2O2 was related to the treatment. However, there were almost no changes in the other groups, suggesting that only NIR irradiation or the AgBiS2 NPs alone could not produce any H2O2.

We further detected the intracellular singlet oxygen concentration by a classic (SOSG) method with minor modifications [22]. As shown in Fig. 2e, almost no green fluorescence could be observed in the control, NIR and AgBiS2 groups after 10 min treatments, suggesting that negligible singlet oxygen was generated in these groups. Interestingly, the cells in the AgBiS2 + NIR group exhibited bright green fluorescence after 5 min of NIR irradiation, and the fluorescence became stronger after 10 min of irradiation, indicating that the AgBiS2 NPs combined with NIR irradiation could produce intracellular singlet oxygen over time.

To detect intracellular ROS generation, we used a DCFH-DA method [23]. Nonfluorescent DCFH-DA can be oxidized to form highly fluorescent DCF by intracellular ROS. As shown in Fig. 2f, the cells in all the groups exhibited invisible green fluorescence before NIR irradiation, indicating that there was almost no ROS production before the NIR irradiation. Impressively, the AgBiS2 + NIR group exhibited increased fluorescence after 5 and 10 min of laser irradiation, indicating that the AgBiS2 NPs could produce a large amount of ROS under NIR irradiation. In contrast, the AgBiS2 group fluorescence dimly after 10 min without irradiation, and almost no fluorescence was observed in the NIR and control groups. The above-mentioned results showed that the AgBiS2 NPs combined with NIR irradiation exhibit excellent photodynamic effects. The ROS induced tumor cell death by reacting with DNA and proteins [24,25]. Hence, the AgBiS2 NPs could ablate the osteosarcoma by the synergistic PTT/PDT effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and tumor ablation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of the AgBiS2 NPs in vitro was measured by a standard CCK-8 method using UMR-106 cells. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the cell viability was slightly lower than 80% when the NP concentration was up to 500 μg mL−1. When the concentrations were lower than 250 μg mL−1, AgBiS2 had a negligible effect on the cells. The influence of the AgBiS2 NPs on the blood was also determined. The DI water group (positive control) turned red because hemoglobin was released from the broken red blood cells (Fig. 3b). In contrast, no visible red color was observed in the AgBiS2 group and PBS group (negative control), indicating the good hemocompatibility of the NPs.

Considering the cell biocompatibility and photothermal/photodynamic effects of the NPs, 250 μg mL−1 AgBiS2 was chosen for the subsequent tumor ablation studies. As shown in Fig. 3c, AgBiS2 combined with NIR irradiation successfully killed the tumor cells, resulting in the significant reduction in the cell viability. However, only the NPs or irradiation were unable to inhibit the fast proliferation of the malignant tumor cells. Live/Dead staining was employed to further confirm the tumor ablation effect. As presented in Fig. 3d, almost all the tumor cells were alive (green fluorescence), and very few dead cells (red fluorescence) were observed in the control, NIR and AgBiS2 groups. In contrast, in the AgBiS2 + NIR group, all the cells were dead due to the effective photothermal/photodynamic effects. These results demonstrated the feasibility of the AgBiS2 NPs for the treatment of an osteosarcoma by the synergistic photothermal/photodynamic effects.

The in vivo tumor ablation experiments were carried out on osteosarcoma-bearing mice by the intratumoral injection of the AgBiS2 NPs (5 mg kg-1) and the application of a 10 min NIR irradiation treatment (808 nm, 1 W cm-2). As shown in Fig. 4 a and c, the tumor volume in the control group increased rapidly due to the extremely high malignancy of the osteosarcoma. The NPs or NIR irradiation alone could not produce photothermal/photodynamic effects in the AgBiS2 and NIR groups, and thus, the tumors grew similarly to those in the control group. Impressively, the growth of the tumors in the AgBiS2 + NIR group was totally inhibited (Fig. 4b), indicating that the AgBiS2-based PTT/PDT had significant therapeutic effects on an osteosarcoma in vivo. Moreover, the mouse body weight (Fig. 4d) in all the groups fluctuated in the range of 18–20 g and were not significantly different, suggesting that the PTT/PDT did not obviously influence the mouse health.

The histological studies were performed on the tumors and main organs to better investigate the PTT/PDT efficiency and histocompatibility. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the tumor tissues maintained a normal structure consisting of spindle-shape tumor cells in the control, NIR and AgBiS2 groups. Remarkably, the tumor tissues became vague, and no intact tumor cells were found in the AgBiS2 + NIR group. Furthermore, the results of H&E staining (Fig. 5b) the main organs were similar among all the groups. Specifically, the metabolic organs were not damaged. The kidneys exhibited clear indication of glomerular disease, and the hepatocytes in the liver were normal. In previous studies, Liu et al. [3] and Li et al. [26] performed blood biochemical analyses 12 h after the injection of Bi2S3 and Bi2Se3 NPs. The results showed that the liver and kidney function makers, including alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), creatinine (CREA), and so on, were similar with those in the control group. In our previous study, we also observed by performing routine blood and biochemical tests that the Bi-based NPs had no obvious effects on the mice 7 days after injection. Additionally, we also studied the metabolism and clearance of the Bi-based NPs [14,27]. We found that the Bi-based NPs were metabolized mainly through the liver and spleen, and the Bi content in the main organs decreased significantly after 7 days. These overall results suggested that the AgBiS2-based PTT/PDT could not only ablate the tumor completely but also avoid causing damage to normal organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo CT imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high K-edge value and good X-ray attenuation properties of Bi (5.74 cm2 g−1 at 100 keV) [2], we measured the CT imaging ability of the AgBiS2 NPs in vitro and in vivo using a micro-CT. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CT signal intensity of the AgBiS2 NPs improved with increasing concentrations. The Hounsfield units (HU) slope was calculated to be 31.95, which was higher than that of the clinical CT contrast agents iobitridol (25.63) and iopromide (16.38) [28]. Moreover, the AgBiS2 NPs also exhibited higher or similar HU values compared to previously reported Au NPs [16] and our previously reported Au nanoruds [14], which had HU values of 22 and 31.26, respectively.

For in vivo CT imaging, the AgBiS2 NPs and NaCl (control) were intratumorally injected into the tumor of the osteosarcoma-bearing mice. Obviously, the tumor site after the injection of the AgBiS2 NPs exhibited a brighter CT signal compared to the signal observed for other soft tissues, while the control group had no signal change after being injected with NaCl (Fig. 6b). Therefore, the AgBiS2 NPs could be used as CT contrast agents for tumor imaging.

Bacterial infection is one of the common complications of tumor treatments due to the immunosuppression caused by chemotherapy [29,30]. Silver-doped biomaterials have exhibited a strong antibacterial activity [31,32], and we tested the antibacterial potential of the AgBiS2 NPs. Clinically derived S. aureus (the most common pathogens found in clinics) were co-cultured with the AgBiS2 NPs. We found that almost all the bacteria were killed by the NPs with concentrations of 500 and 1000 μg mL-1 (Fig. 7 ). Moreover, the NPs showed an antibacterial activity of 90% even with a very low NP concentration (62.5 μg mL-1). Therefore, the AgBiS2 NPs have an excellent antibacterial ability and can minimize infection during treatment.

These above-mentioned results indicated that the AgBiS2 NPs can be used for simultaneous multifunctional tumor therapy and exhibit an excellent PTT/PDT effect, enhanced contrast for CT imaging and a strong antibacterial ability. However, the long-term in vivo toxicity of the AgBiS2 NPs remains to be studied. Moreover, engineering Bi-based NP surfaces with a tumor antibody [14] or targeted peptides [6,27] could achieve precise tumor therapy and real-time CT monitoring, and thus, the in vivo efficiency of the AgBiS2 NPs after surface modification also needs to be further tested.

A facile solvothermal method was used to synthesize AgBiS2. Briefly, 0.1 mmol of bismuth nitrate pentahydrate and 0.1 mmol of silver nitrate were dissolved in 10 mL of octadecene with constant stirring under a vacuum at 100 °C. Next, thioacetamide (0.2 mmol) in 5 mL of degassed oleylamine was quickly injected into a mixed Bi and Ag solution at 100 °C and heated to 180 °C for 30 min. The final NPs were purified three times by precipitation/dissolution with acetone and hexane, and the precipitate (AgBiS2 NPs) was used for the next reaction.

To render the hydrophobic AgBiS2 NPs hydrophilic, Tween-20 was introduced for the surface modification [33]. Briefly, 20 mg of the AgBiS2 NPs and 50 μL of Tween-20 were dissolved in 20 mL of cyclohexane in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min, and then, the NPs were added dropwise to 30 mL of DI water. The final Tween-20-functionalized NPs were obtained after an overnight reaction to completely evaporate the cyclohexane.

The morphology of the AgBiS2 NPs was observed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL 2100, Japan), in which a drop of the NP ethanol suspension was deposited onto a carbon-coated copper grid. The crystal structures and phase purities of the AgBiS2 NPs were investigated by XRD using a Siemens Kristalloflex 810 D-500 X-ray diffractometer (Karslruhe, Germany), and the operating voltage and current were maintained at 40 kV and 40 mA, respectively, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). The optical absorption spectra were obtained by a spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA).

To verify the in vitro photothermal effect of AgBiS2, 400 μL of AgBiS2 aqueous solutions (0, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 μg mL-1) were added to a 48-well plate and were then irradiated by a 808 nm NIR laser (BWT, Beijing, China) at a power density of 1 W cm-2 for 10 min. The temperature changes were measured by a thermocouple microprobe every 10 s. Next, the laser was turned off, the solutions were allowed to cool down to room temperature, and the temperature changes were recorded.

The UMR-106 cells (osteosarcoma cell line) were co-cultured with or without 250 μg mL−1 AgBiS2 NPs for 24 h at 37 °C. The UMR-106 cells without the NPs were set as the control. The other groups were irradiated with or without NIR (808 nm, 1 W cm−2) for 1–10 min. The solutions were tested by a hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) assay kit (Beyotime, China) every minute after NIR irradiation, and the OD values were immediately measured with a microplate reader at 405 nm. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

Intracellular singlet oxygen was detected by a Singlet Oxygen Sensor Green (SOSG) reagent (Meilunbio, China), which was followed by a classic cellular staining method with minor modifications [20]. Briefly, 500 μL of AgBiS2 solutions (250 μg mL-1 in NaCl) or NaCl were added to the UMR-106 cells. After 30 min of co-cultivation, the cells were irradiated with or without NIR (808 nm, 1 W cm−2) for 0, 5 or 10 min. The cells were carefully washed with NaCl, incubated with 500 μL of the disposed SOSG reaction solutions for an additional 2 h in the dark, and then observed with a fluorescence microscope.

Intracellular ROS generation was detected by the DCFH-DA method. The cells were treated as above-mentioned and irradiated with or without NIR (808 nm, 1 W cm−2) for 0, 5 or 10 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed with NaCl and then co-cultured with 500 μL of a DCFH-DA working solution at 37 °C for an additional 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of the cells was observed with a fluorescence microscope.

We investigated the cytotoxicity of the AgBiS2 NPs by a standard Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method. The UMR-106 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h with Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (Gibco, USA) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (Sigma, USA). Next, 100 mL of various concentrations of the AgBiS2 NPs (0, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 μg mL-1) was added. After 24 h of co-culture, the CCK-8 solution (10%) was added to each well and cultured for an additional 2 h, and the optical density (OD) values were measured at 450 nm with a microplate reader (Multiskan GO, Thermal Scientific, USA). Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

Whole blood containing sodium citrate was obtained from healthy people and was diluted with PBS (4:5). Then, 1 mL of AgBiS2 solutions with different concentrations, as above-mentioned, were incubated in tubes at 37 °C for 30 min. Meanwhile, equal amounts of the dilute blood were mixed with 1 mL of pure water and PBS to serve as the positive and negative controls, respectively. Then, 200 μL of the diluted blood was added to the above-mentioned samples and co-cultured for an additional 1 h. After the samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 5 min, the OD values of the supernatants were measured at 545 nm. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

The UMR-106 cells (1 × 104 cells per well) were co-cultured with or without 250 μg mL-1 AgBiS2 NPs. After the cells were treated with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 1 W cm-2, 10 min), the cell viability was determined by a standard CCK-8 assay. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

For the viability Live/Dead staining studies, the cells with different treatments were stained with Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) for 30 min according to the manufacturer's protocol (Sigma, USA). The cells were then imaged using a fluorescence microscope (DMI4000, Leica, Germany).

Male BALB/c nude mice (6-8 weeks old) were purchased from the Medical Experimental Animal Center of Guangdong Province, and all the animal studies were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of General Hospital of Southern Theater Command of PLA. Osteosarcoma models were generated by subcutaneously injecting 5 × 105 UMR-106 cells into the back of the nude mice. When the tumor size reached 5 × 5 mm, the osteosarcoma-bearing nude mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (n = 3) and given the following intratumoral injection treatments: 1) NaCl (control); 2) NaCl + NIR (808 nm, 1 W cm-2, 10 min); 3) AgBiS2 (5 mg kg-1); and 4) AgBiS2 + NIR. The tumor volumes and body weight changes of the mice were measured every other day over the next 2 weeks. The tumor volume was calculated using formula V = ab2/2, where V (mm3) is the volume of the tumor, and a (mm) and b (mm) are the tumor length and tumor width, respectively.

Fourteen days after the treatments, the tumor samples and main organs of the mice, including the heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys and brain, were collected. All the samples were encapsulated in paraffin, cut into 5 μm sections, loaded onto glass slides, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The pathological sections were observed with an inverted microscope (BX51, Olympus, Japan).

To measure the HU value, different concentrations of the AgBiS2 solutions (40, 20, 10, 5, 2.5 and 0 mg/mL) were put into a 24-well plate under X-ray scanning. In vivo scanning was performed on a small mouse X-ray CT (Aloka, Hitachi, Japan) with a scanning resolution of 48 μm, a rotation angle of 360°, and a voltage of 80 kV. Osteosarcoma-bearing mice were intratumorally injected with AgBiS2 (5 mg kg-1) or NaCl (control). Before the injection and 2 h after the injection, the mice were imaged, and the CT images were analyzed using VGStudio MAX 2.2.

The antibacterial ability of the AgBiS2 NPs was evaluated by co-culturing the NPs with the clinically derived S. aureus. Briefly, the bacterial suspensions with concentrations of 106 CFU mL−1 were co-cultured with various concentrations of the AgBiS2 NPs (1000, 500, 250, 125 and 62.5 μg mL−1) in a biochemical incubator at 37 °C. The bacterial suspensions without the NPs were set as the control group. After 24 h, the suspensions were diluted 1000 times by NaCl, and 100 μL of the diluted suspension was spread on a Luria-Broth (LB) agar plate. The number of visible bacterial colonies was counted after incubation at 37 °C for an additional 24 h. The antibacterial rate was calculated by the following formula: antibacterial rate = (Ncontrol - Nsample)/Ncontrol × 100%, where Ncontrol is the average number of bacteria in the control group, and Nsample is the average number of bacteria in the sample groups. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

Data were analyzed by SPSS 19.0 software (IBM, USA). Statistical comparisons amongst the groups were performed using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), which was followed by a post hoc multiple comparison (least significant difference, LSD). The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation. A p-value less than 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant.

In conclusion, multifunctional AgBiS2 NPs were prepared and used for malignant tumor PTT/PDT, CT imaging and anti-infection applications. The AgBiS2 NPs possessed good biocompatibility and could effectively convert a NIR laser into heat. Moreover, the AgBiS2 NPs increased the amounts of H2O2, singlet oxygen and ROS produced under NIR irradiation. Taking advantage of these PTT/PDT-favored features, the AgBiS2 NPs combined with NIR irradiation successfully killed tumor cells in vitro and remarkably inhibited the growth of highly malignant osteosarcoma in vivo. In addition, the AgBiS2 NPs exhibited not only a high HU value, enabling them to serve as excellent CT imaging agents, but also strong antibacterial ability towards clinical bacteria, which can reduce the chance of infection during treatment. Hence, the AgBiS2 NPs could be used as a powerful tool for malignant tumor monitoring and therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by China Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Supporting Program (BX20190150), Presidential Foundation of the Zhujiang Hospital (yzjj2018rc09), Scientific Research Foundation of Southern Medical University (C1051353, PY2018N060), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640776), National Natural Science Foundation of China (81601884, 81902198), Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2014A020215025, 2017B030314139) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2015A030312004).
